title: 11. Training Diagnostics & Debugging
subtitle: CUDA OOM, VRAM Optimization, Gradient Checkpointing, ZeRO & NaN Loss

sections.heading: Diagnostic Frameworks for Large-Scale Model Training
sections.body: The industrialization of LLM training has transitioned the field from experimental science to a high-stakes engineering discipline where the management of physical and numerical constraints is as vital as the architectural configuration. In the regime of trillion-parameter models, hardware failures and numerical instabilities are not occasional anomalies but **mathematical certainties**. [1] Engineering at this scale requires a nuanced perspective on the interplay between GPU memory hierarchy, floating-point physics, and the non-linear dynamics of preference optimization. This deep-dive serves as an exhaustive technical runbook for diagnosing, mitigating, and resolving the most critical bottlenecks in contemporary distributed training.

sections.heading: 1. Physical Constraints and GPU Memory Optimization Strategies

blocks.content: The most frequent barrier in large-scale model development is the **"Runtime Error: CUDA Out of Memory" (OOM)**. To the senior engineer, an OOM is a binary failure indicating a violation of the VRAM capacity—such as the 80 GB HBM2e buffer on an NVIDIA A100 or the 141 GB HBM3e buffer on an H200. [2] Resolving OOM conditions requires a rigorous decomposition of VRAM utilization within the PyTorch autograd engine.

blocks.rootCause: VRAM capacity exceeded by the sum of static model states (weights, gradients, optimizer states) and dynamic activation buffers. A 70B model requires ~1.1 TB of VRAM for full fine-tuning with Adam—nearly 14× the capacity of a single 80 GB GPU. [3]
fixes: Decompose VRAM into static vs. dynamic components to identify the dominant consumer.
Apply Gradient Checkpointing (Section 1.2) for 50-70% activation memory savings at ~33% compute cost.
Use DeepSpeed ZeRO sharding (Section 1.4) to distribute static states across GPUs.
Reduce batch size or sequence length to lower dynamic activation memory.
Apply LoRA/QLoRA to reduce the number of trainable parameters.

sections.heading: 1.1 Taxonomy of VRAM Consumption: Static vs. Dynamic Memory

blocks.content: VRAM consumption is physically partitioned into **static model states** and **dynamic activation buffers**. [4] Static memory remains constant throughout a training iteration, while dynamic memory fluctuates based on sequence length ($s$) and batch size ($B$). [4]

In a standard training loop utilizing the **AdamW** optimizer, memory requirements are substantially higher than the raw parameter count suggests. A model with $P$ parameters stored in 16-bit half-precision (FP16 or BF16) requires $2P$ bytes for weights. [3] Gradients consume another $2P$ bytes. [3] However, the AdamW optimizer maintains two state vectors per parameter—the **first moment** (momentum) and the **second moment** (variance)—which must be stored in 32-bit (FP32) to ensure convergence, consuming $4P$ bytes each. [6] Furthermore, a master copy of weights in FP32 is maintained to prevent loss of small gradient updates due to rounding, consuming an additional $4P$ bytes. [6]

blocks.title: Static VRAM Decomposition for Full Fine-Tuning with Adam
The 16× Rule
blocks.content: Full fine-tuning with Adam requires **16 bytes per parameter** in total static memory. A 70B parameter model demands approximately **1.1 TB of VRAM** just for static states—nearly 14× the capacity of a single 80 GB GPU. [3] This engineering reality necessitates advanced distributed strategies.

Activation memory represents the dynamic segment that typically triggers OOMs. It consists of the intermediate results saved during the forward pass to facilitate the partial derivatives of the backward pass. [4] For deep networks like Llama 3 (80 layers), the cumulative storage creates linear growth in memory that quickly outstrips hardware capacity. [3]

**Gradient checkpointing** (activation recomputation) reduces this burden by trading computation time for memory space. [4] In a standard backward pass, the memory complexity of activations is $O(L)$, where $L$ is the number of layers. By saving only 'checkpoints' and recomputing intermediate values, we achieve sublinear complexity. [17]
Consider a network with $L$ layers divided into $k$ segments. The memory cost $M(k)$ consists of the stored checkpoints and the active recomputation buffer for the current segment:

blocks.label: Gradient Checkpointing Memory
blocks.description: Where k is the number of checkpoints and L/k is the size of each recomputation segment.

blocks.content: To minimize memory, we find the stationary point with respect to $k$:

blocks.label: Optimal Checkpoint Interval
blocks.description: Checkpointing every √L-th layer reduces memory complexity to O(√L), increasing compute time by approximately 33% (one extra forward pass) while providing 50-70% VRAM savings. [17]

blocks.title: Standard vs. Gradient Checkpointing Memory Layout
blocks.code:
graph TD
  subgraph Standard["Standard: All Activations Saved"]
    F1[Layer 1 Act] --> F2[Layer 2 Act]
    F2 --> F3[Layer 3 Act]
    F3 --> F4[Layer 4 Act]
  end
  subgraph Checkpointed["Checkpointed: Only Checkpoints Saved"]
    C1["Checkpoint 1 (saved)"] --> D2["Layer 2 (discarded)"]
    D2 --> D3["Layer 3 (discarded)"]
    D3 --> C4["Checkpoint 4 (saved)"]
    C1 -."Recompute during backward".-> R2[Layer 2 Act]
    R2 -.-> R3[Layer 3 Act]
  end

blocks.content: Modern PyTorch standardizes on **non-reentrant checkpointing** (`use_reentrant=False`) for better compatibility with the autograd engine and support for complex metadata preservation. [20]

blocks.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint

class TransformerLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, nhead):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, nhead)
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_model * 4),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(d_model * 4, d_model)
        )
        self.ln1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.ln2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x):
        def block(hidden_states):
            res = hidden_states
            attn_out, _ = self.attn(hidden_states, hidden_states, hidden_states)
            hidden_states = self.ln1(res + attn_out)
            res = hidden_states
            hidden_states = self.ln2(res + self.mlp(hidden_states))
            return hidden_states

        # SOTA: use_reentrant=False handles RNG states
        # and dropout masks correctly
        return checkpoint(block, x, use_reentrant=False)

blocks.content: When local optimization is insufficient, engineers employ the **Zero Redundancy Optimizer (ZeRO)** to shard the model state across distributed GPUs. [10] ZeRO operates in three stages to eliminate the redundancy of replicated model parameters in data parallelism. [10]

items.label: ZeRO-1 (P_os)
items.detail: Partitions only the optimizer states. Provides a **4× reduction** in VRAM with minimal communication overhead. [11]

items.label: ZeRO-2 (P_os+g)
items.detail: Partitions gradients and optimizer states, providing an **8× reduction**. [11]

items.label: ZeRO-3 (P_os+g+p)
items.detail: Partitions parameters, gradients, and optimizer states. Materializes a full layer's parameters on-demand via an **All-Gather** collective and discards them immediately after computation. Peak memory depends on the largest layer rather than the entire model. [10]

blocks.title: ZeRO-3 All-Gather and Discard Flow
blocks.code:
flowchart LR
  subgraph GPU_0
    P0["Params Shard 0"]
    G0["Grads Shard 0"]
    O0["Optim Shard 0"]
  end
  subgraph GPU_1
    P1["Params Shard 1"]
    G1["Grads Shard 1"]
    O1["Optim Shard 1"]
  end
  GPU_0 -->|"All-Gather Params"| LW[Layer Workspace]
  GPU_1 -->|"All-Gather Params"| LW
  LW -->|"Forward/Backward"| Result
  Result -->|"Discard Full Params"| Shards[Return to Shards]

blocks.content: Numerical instability manifests as **Loss Divergence** (NaN bugs), where mathematical values overflow the hardware's representable range. [1] This is often a mismatch between the network's dynamic range and the bit-depth format. [13]

sections.heading: 2.1 The FP16 Overflow Crisis and BF16 Solution

blocks.content: Standard FP16 (Half-Precision) allocates 5 bits for the exponent and 10 bits for the mantissa. [13] This caps its maximum representable value at **65,504**. [13] In deep LLMs, activation and gradient spikes frequently exceed this threshold, yielding 'Inf' values that result in NaNs during weight updates. [1]

**BF16 (Brain Floating Point)** resolves this by reallocating bits: 8 bits for the exponent (matching FP32's dynamic range of up to $\sim 3.4 \times 10^{38}$) and only 7 bits for the mantissa. [26] This engineering tradeoff sacrifices precision for dynamic range. For LLM training, where gradient magnitudes can span many orders of magnitude, the wider exponent range is far more valuable than mantissa precision. [27] The reduced mantissa precision has negligible impact on final model quality because stochastic gradient descent is inherently noisy. [26]

blocks.symptom: NaN loss during training, Inf values in gradients, or sudden loss divergence
blocks.rootCause: FP16 overflow — activations or gradients exceed the 65,504 maximum representable value of FP16 format. The 5-bit exponent provides insufficient dynamic range for deep LLM training. [13]
fixes: **Switch to BF16**: 8 exponent bits provide FP32's dynamic range, eliminating overflow. This is the single most impactful fix for NaN loss. [26]
**If BF16 unavailable** (pre-Ampere GPUs): Use mixed-precision training with FP16 + dynamic loss scaling (see Section 2.2).
**Apply gradient clipping**: `torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)` prevents gradient spikes from propagating.
**Maintain FP32 master weights**: Ensures small gradient updates aren't lost to rounding in half-precision. [6]
**Check learning rate**: Excessively high LR amplifies gradient magnitudes. Reduce LR or use warmup.
**Inspect data**: Corrupted or extreme outlier examples can cause single-batch spikes that cascade into NaN.

sections.heading: 2.2 Mixed Precision Training and Dynamic Loss Scaling

blocks.content: When BF16 is not available (pre-Ampere hardware), engineers rely on **mixed precision training** with FP16. The core idea is to maintain FP32 master weights while performing forward and backward passes in FP16 for speed. However, FP16's limited range means small gradients can **underflow** to zero, causing training to stall.

**Dynamic Loss Scaling** addresses this by multiplying the loss by a large scale factor before backpropagation, shifting the gradient distribution into FP16's representable range. PyTorch's `GradScaler` implements this automatically:

blocks.code:
import torch
from torch.amp import autocast, GradScaler

scaler = GradScaler()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)

for batch in dataloader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()

    # Forward pass in FP16
    with autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=torch.float16):
        loss = model(batch)

    # Backward pass with scaled loss
    scaler.scale(loss).backward()

    # Unscale gradients, then clip
    scaler.unscale_(optimizer)
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)

    # Step with scaler (skips update if Inf/NaN detected)
    scaler.step(optimizer)
    scaler.update()  # Adjusts scale factor dynamically

blocks.content: The `GradScaler` maintains an internal scale factor that starts high (e.g., 65536) and adjusts dynamically. If an Inf or NaN is detected in the unscaled gradients, the optimizer step is **skipped entirely** and the scale factor is halved. If no overflow occurs for a sustained period, the scale factor is doubled. This feedback loop finds the optimal scale for the model's gradient distribution.

blocks.title: BF16 Eliminates the Need for Loss Scaling
blocks.content: On A100+ hardware, BF16 is strictly preferred over FP16+GradScaler. BF16's FP32-equivalent dynamic range means overflow and underflow are virtually impossible, removing the complexity of loss scaling entirely while maintaining equivalent training speed. [26]

Even with BF16, gradient explosions can occur due to architectural instabilities or adversarial batches. **Gradient clipping** is a universal safeguard that caps the magnitude of gradients before the optimizer step.

items.title: Global Norm Clipping (Recommended)
items.description: Computes the L2 norm across all parameters and scales all gradients uniformly if the total norm exceeds `max_norm`. Preserves relative gradient directions.
pros: Standard in all major LLM training codebases (Megatron, DeepSpeed)
cons: Requires an All-Reduce to compute global norm in distributed settings

items.title: Per-Parameter Value Clipping
items.description: Clamps each individual gradient value to [-clip_value, +clip_value] independently.
cons: Distorts gradient direction — different parameters are clipped by different ratios

blocks.code:
# Global Norm Clipping (RECOMMENDED for LLMs)
total_norm = torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(
    model.parameters(),
    max_norm=1.0  # Typical range: 0.5 - 2.0
)
# Log the pre-clip norm for monitoring
if total_norm > 1.0:
    print(f"Gradient clipped: {total_norm:.2f} -> 1.0")

# Per-Parameter Value Clipping (NOT recommended)
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_value_(model.parameters(), clip_value=0.5)

blocks.title: Monitor Gradient Norm as a Health Metric
blocks.content: If gradient norm is **consistently being clipped** (e.g., > 90% of steps), the learning rate is likely too high or the data has quality issues. If gradient norm **occasionally spikes** but self-corrects, clipping is doing its job. If gradient norm **diverges monotonically**, the training run is unstable — stop, diagnose, and reduce LR.

sections.heading: 2.4 Convergence Diagnostics: Reading the Loss Curve

blocks.content: Beyond preventing NaN crashes, engineers must interpret the loss curve to diagnose subtler training failures. The loss curve is the single most important diagnostic signal in LLM training.

blocks.title: Loss Curve Diagnostic Patterns
rows: Loss shoots to Inf then NaN
FP16 overflow or learning rate too high
Switch to BF16; reduce LR; add gradient clipping
Loss stops decreasing after initial drop
Learning rate too low or data quality issue
Increase LR; check data pipeline for duplicates/corruption
Loss jumps up and down without convergence
Batch size too small or LR too high
Increase batch size (via gradient accumulation); reduce LR
Insufficient data or too many epochs
Add regularization; reduce training steps; increase data diversity
All GPUs report same loss values
Correct behavior in DDP — this is expected
No fix needed — DDP synchronizes gradients
Gradient sync broken or NCCL communication failure
Check NCCL environment variables; verify All-Reduce is working

blocks.content: When fine-tuning a pretrained LLM on domain-specific data, **catastrophic forgetting** causes the model to lose its general capabilities. The model overwrites the broad knowledge embedded in its weights with the narrow distribution of the fine-tuning dataset.

blocks.symptom: Model excels on fine-tuning task but loses ability to perform general chat, reasoning, or other previously mastered capabilities
blocks.rootCause: Full fine-tuning modifies all parameters, overwriting the pretrained weight distribution with the narrow fine-tuning distribution. The degree of forgetting is proportional to the divergence between pretraining and fine-tuning data distributions.
fixes: **Use LoRA/QLoRA**: Freeze base weights and only train low-rank adapters (~0.1-1% of parameters). This is the primary defense.
**Data mixing**: Blend domain data with a fraction (5-10%) of general-purpose data to maintain broad capabilities.
**Reduce learning rate**: Use 1/10th of the pretraining LR (e.g., 2e-5 instead of 2e-4) to limit weight drift.
**Early stopping**: Monitor both task-specific and general benchmarks. Stop when the task metric plateaus, before general capability degrades.
**Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC)**: Add a regularization term that penalizes large changes to parameters deemed important for prior tasks.

blocks.content: At scale, the probability of hardware failure during a training run becomes near-certain. A 1000-GPU training run with 99.9% per-GPU reliability over 24 hours still faces a **63% chance of at least one failure** ($1 - 0.999^{1000} \approx 0.63$). Engineers must design resilient training pipelines.

blocks.symptom: Training hangs without error, GPU utilization drops to 0%, or NCCL timeout errors
blocks.rootCause: NCCL collective operations (All-Reduce, All-Gather) require all ranks to participate. If one GPU fails silently (e.g., ECC memory error, PCIe link degradation), other GPUs wait indefinitely for the failed rank.
fixes: **Set NCCL timeout**: `os.environ['NCCL_TIMEOUT'] = '1800'` (30 min) to convert hangs into actionable errors.
**Enable NCCL debugging**: `NCCL_DEBUG=INFO` logs all collective operations and identifies the stalled rank.
**Checkpoint frequently**: Save model state every N steps so recovery from hardware failures loses minimal progress.
**Use elastic training** (e.g., `torchrun --nproc_per_node=auto`): Automatically resumes training on remaining healthy GPUs.
**Monitor GPU health**: Track ECC errors, temperature, and memory utilization. Preemptively replace GPUs with rising ECC counts.

blocks.symptom: Training runs but throughput is 50-80% lower than expected
blocks.rootCause: Communication bottleneck — All-Reduce operations dominate the iteration time, typically caused by slow interconnects, misconfigured NCCL, or imbalanced GPU memory forcing excessive ZeRO-3 All-Gather operations.
fixes: **Profile with `torch.profiler`**: Identify whether compute or communication dominates each step.
**Overlap communication with compute**: Enable `overlap_comm=True` in DeepSpeed ZeRO config.
**Use gradient accumulation**: Increase effective batch size with fewer All-Reduce operations per sample.
**Verify NVLink/InfiniBand**: Run `nvidia-smi topo -m` to confirm GPUs are connected via NVLink, not PCIe. Run `ibstat` for InfiniBand health.
**Reduce ZeRO stage**: ZeRO-3 has maximum communication overhead. If VRAM allows, fall back to ZeRO-2 or ZeRO-1.

points: **CUDA OOM** is diagnosed by decomposing VRAM into static (weights + gradients + optimizer states) and dynamic (activations) components.
Full fine-tuning with Adam requires **16 bytes per parameter** — a 70B model needs ~1.1 TB of static VRAM alone.
**Gradient Checkpointing** trades ~33% extra compute for 50-70% activation memory savings. The optimal checkpoint interval is every **√L** layers.
Always use `use_reentrant=False` for PyTorch gradient checkpointing to correctly handle RNG states and dropout masks. [20]
**DeepSpeed ZeRO** eliminates data-parallel redundancy: Stage 1 → 4× savings, Stage 2 → 8× savings, Stage 3 → memory scales with largest layer, not total model.
**BF16 is strictly preferred over FP16** for LLM training — its 8-bit exponent matches FP32's dynamic range, preventing overflow (max 65,504 in FP16 vs ~3.4×10³⁸ in BF16). [26]
NaN loss is typically caused by FP16 overflow. Fix: switch to BF16, apply gradient clipping (`max_norm=1.0`), and maintain FP32 master weights.
**Dynamic Loss Scaling** (GradScaler) is required for FP16 but unnecessary with BF16.
**Catastrophic forgetting** during fine-tuning is prevented by LoRA, data mixing, and reduced learning rates.
At 1000-GPU scale, hardware failure is a **mathematical certainty** — frequent checkpointing and NCCL timeout configuration are essential.
